request.prompt: Build a CI/CD pipeline for a Next.js app with GitHub Actions, testing, staging deploy, and production release

response.message: Mission received. Building CI/CD pipeline for Next.js app with GitHub Actions, testing, staging deploy, and production release.

nodes.label: GitHub Push
nodes.description: Trigger on push to main or PR to main branch.
nodes.content: Trigger: push to main branch or pull_request targeting main. Path: .github/workflows/ci-cd.yml

nodes.label: Install & Build
nodes.description: Checkout code, install dependencies, and build Next.js app.
nodes.content:
Steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
  with:
    node-version: '20'
- run: npm ci
- run: npm run build

nodes.label: Unit & Integration Tests
nodes.description: Run Jest/Vitest unit tests and integration tests.
nodes.content:
Steps:
- run: npm test -- --coverage
- run: npm run test:integration (if configured)
- uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4 (optional)

nodes.label: Lint & Type Check
nodes.description: Run ESLint, Prettier, and TypeScript type checking.
nodes.content:
Steps:
- run: npm run lint
- run: npm run type-check
- run: npm run format:check

nodes.label: Staging Deploy
nodes.description: Deploy built app to staging environment (Vercel/Netlify/AWS).

nodes.label: Staging E2E Tests
nodes.description: Run end-to-end tests against staging deployment.
nodes.content:
Steps:
- run: npm run test:e2e -- --baseUrl=https://staging.myapp.com
- uses: cypress-io/github-action@v6 (if using Cypress)

nodes.label: Manual Approval Gate
nodes.description: Manual approval required before production release.
nodes.content:
GitHub Environments:
- environment: production
  required reviewers: team-lead
- Checks: staging tests passed, no critical issues.

nodes.label: Production Release
nodes.description: Deploy to production after approval.

nodes.label: Post-Deploy Smoke Test
nodes.description: Run quick health checks on production deployment.
nodes.content:
Steps:
- run: curl -f https://myapp.com/api/health
- run: npm run test:smoke -- --baseUrl=https://myapp.com

checks: ✗ Missing "input" category (found: trigger, action, test, review)
✗ Missing "output" category (found: trigger, action, test, review)

request.prompt: Create a content publishing workflow: draft article, AI review, editor approval, SEO optimization, publish to CMS, social media distribution

response.message: Mission received. Building content publishing workflow with draft, AI review, editor approval, SEO optimization, CMS publish, and social distribution.

nodes.label: Article Draft
nodes.description: Initial draft of the article content.
nodes.content:
# Article Title

## Introduction
This is the draft content. It includes the main body, key points, and any initial media references.

## Sections
- Section 1: Main argument
- Section 2: Supporting evidence
- Section 3: Conclusion

## Notes
Awaiting review and optimization.

nodes.label: AI Content Review
nodes.description: AI analyzes draft for clarity, tone, and basic SEO.
nodes.content:
AI Review Report:
- Clarity Score: 85/100
- Tone: Professional, matches brand voice.
- Readability: Grade 10 level.
- Basic SEO: Missing meta description, keyword density low.
- Recommendations: Add meta description, include primary keyword in first paragraph, shorten sentences for readability.

nodes.label: Editor Approval
nodes.description: Human editor reviews and approves the draft.
nodes.content:
Editor Approval Checklist:
- [x] Fact-checking complete
- [x] Grammar and spelling reviewed
- [x] Brand voice alignment confirmed
- [x] Legal and compliance check passed
- [ ] Final approval granted

Approval Status: PENDING - Awaiting final sign-off after SEO updates.

nodes.label: SEO Optimization
nodes.description: Optimize article for search engines.
nodes.content:
SEO Optimization Tasks:
1. Primary Keyword: 'content publishing workflow'
2. Meta Title: Optimized Content Publishing Workflow: From Draft to Distribution
3. Meta Description: A step-by-step guide to efficient content publishing with AI review, editor approval, SEO, and social distribution.
4. URL Slug: /content-publishing-workflow-guide
5. Header Tags: H1, H2 structured with keywords
6. Image Alt Text: Added for all media
7. Internal Linking: Added 3 relevant internal links
8. Readability: Adjusted to Grade 8 level

nodes.label: CMS Publish
nodes.description: Publish the optimized article to the Content Management System.
nodes.content:
CMS Publish Configuration:
- CMS: WordPress
- Status: Publish
- Schedule: Immediate
- Categories: Content Strategy, Workflow
- Tags: publishing, SEO, AI, social media
- Featured Image: Uploaded and set
- Author: Assigned
- Revision Saved: Yes

nodes.label: Social Media Distribution
nodes.description: Distribute the published article across social platforms.
nodes.content:
Social Distribution Plan:
- Twitter: Thread with key takeaways, link to article
- LinkedIn: Professional post highlighting workflow efficiency
- Facebook: Engaging post with visual preview
- Instagram: Carousel with workflow steps, link in bio
- Scheduling: Hootsuite used for timed posts over 48 hours
- Tracking: UTM parameters added for analytics

nodes.label: Publishing Trigger
nodes.description: Initiates the workflow when a new draft is ready.
nodes.content:
Trigger Condition: New article draft submitted to the workflow system.
Action: Automatically route to AI Content Review node.

nodes.label: Content Calendar
nodes.description: Tracks publishing schedule and status.
nodes.content:
Content Calendar Entry:
- Article ID: CP-2023-10-001
- Title: Optimized Content Publishing Workflow
- Draft Date: 2023-10-05
- AI Review Date: 2023-10-06
- Editor Approval Date: 2023-10-07
- SEO Date: 2023-10-08
- Publish Date: 2023-10-09
- Social Distribution: 2023-10-09 to 2023-10-11
- Status: In Progress

request.prompt: Design an incident response workflow: alert detection, triage, investigation, mitigation, post-mortem, and preventive action

response.message: Aha! A case of digital disturbance, mon ami! The incident response workflow — a systematic investigation of technological malfeasance. I shall construct this procedural masterpiece with the precision of a detective's notebook. Voilà! The Incident Response Investigation Protocol, complete with six distinct phases of inquiry and remediation.

nodes.label: Alert Detection System
nodes.description: Initial anomaly detection through monitoring tools, SIEM alerts, or user reports
nodes.content: Continuous monitoring of: 1) System logs (failed logins, unusual access patterns) 2) Network traffic (DDoS signatures, data exfiltration) 3) Application performance (latency spikes, error rate increases) 4) Security tool alerts (IDS/IPS, antivirus, WAF). Thresholds configured for automatic escalation when anomalies exceed baseline by 3 standard deviations.

nodes.label: Triage & Classification
nodes.description: Initial assessment of incident severity, scope, and categorization
nodes.content: Triage Protocol: 1) Determine impact level (Critical/High/Medium/Low) based on affected systems, data sensitivity, and user impact 2) Classify incident type (Security breach, System outage, Data corruption, Performance degradation) 3) Assign incident ID and create initial ticket 4) Notify appropriate response team based on classification matrix. SLA: Critical - 15 minutes, High - 30 minutes, Medium - 2 hours, Low - 4 hours.

nodes.label: Investigation Command Center
nodes.description: Centralized coordination point for evidence collection and analysis
nodes.content: Investigation Toolkit: 1) Forensic data collection (memory dumps, disk images, network captures) 2) Timeline reconstruction using log correlation 3) IOC (Indicators of Compromise) identification 4) Root cause analysis methodology (5 Whys, Fishbone diagram) 5) Evidence chain of custody documentation. All findings documented in centralized incident log with timestamps and investigator signatures.

nodes.label: Mitigation Actions
nodes.description: Immediate containment and remediation steps to limit damage
nodes.content: Containment Strategies: 1) Isolation (network segmentation, system quarantine) 2) Access revocation (disable compromised accounts, API keys) 3) Patch deployment (emergency security updates) 4) Traffic rerouting (failover to backup systems) 5) Data restoration from clean backups. Each action requires approval from incident commander with documented rationale and risk assessment.

nodes.label: Post-Mortem Analysis
nodes.description: Comprehensive review documenting what happened and lessons learned
nodes.content: Post-Mortem Report Template: 1) Executive summary 2) Timeline of events 3) Root cause analysis 4) Impact assessment (financial, reputational, operational) 5) Response effectiveness evaluation 6) Action items with owners and deadlines 7) Recommendations for process improvements. Must be completed within 5 business days of incident resolution.

nodes.label: Preventive Action Implementation
nodes.description: Systemic changes to prevent recurrence of similar incidents
nodes.content: Prevention Framework: 1) Update security policies and procedures 2) Implement new monitoring rules based on incident patterns 3) Enhance training programs for relevant teams 4) Architectural improvements (redundancy, failover mechanisms) 5) Regular penetration testing and vulnerability scanning schedule 6) Update incident response playbooks with new scenarios. All preventive actions tracked in improvement backlog with quarterly review.

nodes.label: Incident Response Playbook
nodes.description: Living document containing procedures, contacts, and escalation paths
nodes.content: Playbook Sections: 1) Team roles and responsibilities (Incident Commander, Communications Lead, Technical Lead) 2) Communication templates (internal, customer, regulatory) 3) Escalation matrix with contact information 4) Tool-specific procedures for common scenarios 5) Legal and compliance requirements checklist 6) Post-incident notification requirements. Updated after each major incident with new learnings.

request.prompt: What makes a good deployment pipeline?

response.message: On it. A good deployment pipeline is automated, reliable, fast, and secure. It integrates version control, automated testing, infrastructure-as-code, monitoring, and rollback capabilities. Key elements: CI/CD automation, environment consistency, security scanning, observability, and clear approval gates.

request.prompt: How should I structure a code review process?

response.message: Ah, mon ami, a most excellent question! The structure of a code review process is like a well-orchestrated investigation—each step must be deliberate, each clue examined. Let me put my little grey cells to work and design a workflow that ensures quality, collaboration, and efficiency. Voilà!

nodes.label: Developer Completes Feature
nodes.description: Initiates the review process when a developer finishes a feature or fix.
nodes.content: Developer pushes code to a feature branch and creates a pull request (PR) in the repository (e.g., GitHub, GitLab). The PR includes a clear title, description linking to the issue/ticket, and any testing notes.

nodes.label: Automated Checks
nodes.description: Runs automated tests and linting to catch obvious issues.
nodes.content: CI/CD pipeline triggers on PR creation: runs unit tests, integration tests, static analysis (e.g., ESLint, SonarQube), and security scans. Results are posted as status checks. Must pass before manual review.

nodes.label: Assign Reviewers
nodes.description: Policy for selecting appropriate reviewers based on code changes.
nodes.content: Auto-assign reviewers using CODEOWNERS file or team rotation. Include at least 2 reviewers: one from the same team (domain expert) and one from a different team (fresh perspective). Use tools like GitHub's auto-assign or custom bots.

nodes.label: Reviewer Analysis
nodes.description: Reviewers examine the code for quality, correctness, and adherence to standards.
nodes.content: Reviewers check: 1. Code correctness and logic, 2. Adherence to style guide and best practices, 3. Test coverage and edge cases, 4. Security and performance implications, 5. Documentation updates. Use inline comments for specific lines.

nodes.label: Discussion & Iteration
nodes.description: State where feedback is addressed through comments and revisions.
nodes.content: Developer responds to comments, makes necessary changes, and pushes updates. Reviewers re-examine after updates. Use threaded discussions to resolve disagreements; escalate to tech lead if deadlocked. Aim for resolution within 24-48 hours.

nodes.label: Approval & Merge
nodes.description: Final approval and merging of the code into the main branch.
nodes.content: After all reviewers approve (e.g., 2+ approvals), and automated checks pass, the PR is merged. Use squash or rebase merging as per team policy. Post-merge, CI/CD deploys to staging environment.

nodes.label: Post-Merge Validation
nodes.description: Validation in staging environment to ensure no regressions.
nodes.content: Run smoke tests and integration tests in staging. Monitor logs and metrics for anomalies. If issues arise, rollback or hotfix immediately. Notify the team via Slack/email.

nodes.label: Review Metrics & Retrospective
nodes.description: Artifact capturing metrics and lessons learned for process improvement.
nodes.content: Collect metrics: review cycle time, defect rate, reviewer load. Hold a monthly retrospective to discuss pain points (e.g., too many comments, slow reviews). Update review guidelines and training as needed.

request.prompt: Write a technical specification for a user authentication system with OAuth2, JWT tokens, and role-based access control

response.message: Mission received. Building technical specification workflow for OAuth2/JWT/RBAC authentication system.

nodes.label: Requirements Input
nodes.description: Stakeholder and business requirements for authentication system.
nodes.content: Business requires a secure, scalable user authentication system supporting OAuth2 for third-party logins, JWT for stateless sessions, and role-based access control (RBAC) for internal and external users. Must comply with GDPR, SOC2. Support multi-tenancy.

nodes.label: Auth System Trigger
nodes.description: Initiates the authentication flow.
nodes.content: User attempts to access protected resource or initiates login via UI/API. System intercepts request, checks for valid session/JWT, and triggers appropriate auth flow (OAuth2 redirect, local login, token refresh).

nodes.label: OAuth2 Provider Spec
nodes.description: Detailed specification for OAuth2 integration.
nodes.content: Integrate with OAuth2 providers (Google, GitHub, custom). Use Authorization Code flow with PKCE for public clients. Redirect URIs must be whitelisted. Store provider configurations (client ID, secret, scopes) securely. Handle OAuth2 callbacks, exchange code for access/ID tokens. Map OAuth2 claims to internal user identity.

nodes.label: JWT Token Engine
nodes.description: Specification for JWT issuance, validation, and management.
nodes.content: Generate JWTs upon successful authentication. Use RS256 (RSA) signatures. Payload: sub (user ID), exp (expiry), iat (issued at), roles, tenant ID. Token lifetime: 15 minutes access token, 7 days refresh token. Store refresh tokens hashed in DB. Provide token refresh endpoint. Blacklist compromised tokens via token ID (jti) in Redis.

nodes.label: RBAC Policy Module
nodes.description: Role-based access control policy definitions and enforcement.
nodes.content: Define roles: SuperAdmin, Admin, Editor, Viewer, ExternalUser. Permissions structured as resource:action (e.g., document:read, user:delete). Roles map to permission sets. Policies evaluated per request via middleware. Support role inheritance. Store policies in database with caching layer.

nodes.label: Security & Compliance Review
nodes.description: Security audit and compliance validation.
nodes.content: Review for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities. Ensure HTTPS everywhere, secure cookie flags (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite). Audit logs for all auth events. GDPR: data minimization, right to erasure. SOC2: access controls, change management. Penetration testing required pre-deployment.

nodes.label: Deployment & Monitoring
nodes.description: Deployment steps and operational monitoring.
nodes.content: Deploy as containerized microservice (Docker/K8s). Environment variables for secrets. Use Vault for key management. Monitor: auth success/failure rates, token issuance rate, latency per endpoint (login, token refresh, OAuth2 callback). Alerts on anomaly detection (brute force, token theft patterns).

nodes.label: Technical Specification Output
nodes.description: Final compiled technical specification document.
nodes.content: Technical Specification v1.0: User Authentication System. Components: OAuth2 Gateway, JWT Engine, RBAC Policy Service, User Identity Store, Audit Logger. APIs detailed: /auth/login, /auth/oauth/callback, /auth/refresh, /auth/logout, /auth/roles. Data models: User, Role, Permission, Token. Security: encryption at rest, TLS 1.3, rate limiting, IP allowlisting for admin endpoints. Scalability: stateless JWT, Redis cache, load-balanced auth service. Compliance: GDPR, SOC2 controls documented.

request.prompt: I need to onboard new employees

response.message: Mission received. Building new employee onboarding workflow.

nodes.label: Onboarding Request
nodes.description: HR or hiring manager submits new hire details
nodes.content: Trigger: New hire confirmed with start date, role, department, manager assigned.

nodes.label: HR Setup
nodes.description: HR creates accounts, processes paperwork, schedules orientation
nodes.content:
Actions: - Enter employee into HRIS
- Generate offer letter and contracts
- Set up payroll and benefits enrollment
- Schedule Day 1 orientation session
- Assign onboarding buddy

nodes.label: IT Provisioning
nodes.description: IT provisions hardware, software, and access
nodes.content:
Actions: - Issue laptop/equipment
- Create email and Slack account
- Set up VPN and required software licenses
- Grant role-based system access (GitHub, Jira, etc.)

nodes.label: Manager Prep
nodes.description: Manager prepares team intro and 30-60-90 day plan
nodes.content:
Actions: - Draft 30-60-90 day goals
- Schedule team introductions
- Prepare first assignments
- Set up recurring 1:1s

nodes.label: Day 1 Orientation
nodes.description: New hire attends orientation, completes initial setup
nodes.content:
State: Employee completes:
- HR orientation (policies, benefits)
- IT setup and security training
- Company culture intro
- Meet onboarding buddy

nodes.label: First Week Tasks
nodes.description: Structured checklist for week 1
nodes.content:
Artifact: Checklist
- Complete required trainings
- Set up development environment
- Submit first code review or task
- Attend team meetings
- Meet with key stakeholders

nodes.label: 30-Day Review
nodes.description: Manager and HR check-in on progress and feedback
nodes.content:
Review: Formal check-in covering:
- Role clarity and goals
- Team integration
- Resource needs
- Initial performance feedback
- Adjustments to onboarding plan

nodes.label: Onboarding Complete
nodes.description: Employee fully onboarded, process closed
nodes.content: Output: Employee is productive, integrated, and has completed all required onboarding steps. Process marked complete in HRIS.

request.prompt: Tell me about the current state of this workflow

response.message: Ah, mon ami! I have examined the scene with the utmost care. The current state of this workflow is... *dramatic pause*... completely empty! There are no nodes, no edges, no clues to follow. It is a blank canvas, a fresh case file, a mystery yet to unfold. The little grey cells are eager to begin the investigation. If you wish to build something, simply give the word, and I shall construct a workflow worthy of Hercule Poirot himself. Très intéressant, non?
